commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content: Arphad, or Arad, an island and city on the continent, (Calmet) near Tyre. Of Ana, "of "is not expressed in the Vulgate, (Haydock) and it may be explained as if Ana and Ava were idols of Sepharvaim. (Menochius) But they are commonly supposed to be cities. (Haydock) Ana is probably a city (Du Hamel) built on both sides of the Euphrates, four days' journey from Bagdat. Isaias does not specify these cities in the parallel passage, but they are found in the letter addressed to Ezechias, Isaias xxxvii. Samaria, or the inhabitants who had come from distant parts, and had perhaps revolted. We do not however find that Sennacherib had conquered them, nor does the pretend that all these conquests were made by himself. (Calmet) He gives part of the honour to his ancestors, chap. , and 2 Paralipomenon xxxii. But he asserts that all the gods of the respective countries of Samaria, had yielded to his superior force. (Haydock) Strange infatuation in a man who looked upon the idols as gods. They are in effect nothing, 1 Corinthians viii. But as their votaries were of a different persuasion, ought they not to have acted and spoken consistently. Yet Suetonius (Caius, c. ) informs us, that "on the day when Germanicus died, the temples were stoned, the altars of the gods overturned, the domestic lares thrown out by some into the open air "all to express their grief and indignation at the gods, for not preserving his life.

methodology.step_3: Use Firecrawl to get full content from 'Go to Commentary' links
methodology.step_4: Fallback to BeautifulSoup for accordion content if needed
